primary_diagnosis.name: Centralised chronic low back pain (nociplastic pain) — acute-on-chronic flare

pathophysiology_summary: This is a flare of centralised (nociplastic) chronic low back pain — pain arising from altered central nervous system processing rather than new tissue damage. Central sensitisation amplifies dorsal-horn signalling (wind-up) and impairs descending inhibitory modulation, so ordinary, non-noxious input is perceived as severe pain: hence diffuse, non-dermatomal burning, florid allodynia (light touch felt as excruciating), and pain markedly out of proportion to prior MRI findings. Peripheral analgesics (paracetamol, NSAIDs) are predictably ineffective because the driver is central gain, not peripheral nociception. Acute psychological distress, catastrophising and hyperventilation further up-regulate the sensitised system, producing tachycardia, tachypnoea and mildly raised blood pressure that reflect the pain/anxiety state — NOT a structural or cardiovascular emergency. The correct target is central modulation (a low-dose nocturnal tricyclic such as amitriptyline) plus non-drug down-regulation (paced activity, diaphragmatic breathing, heat, pain psychology). The mandatory safety task is to screen for and safety-net the serious mimics — cauda equina syndrome, an acute structural/neurological lesion, and OTC analgesic overdose — which are screened NEGATIVE here.

differential.diagnosis: Centralised chronic low back pain flare (nociplastic pain)
differential.evidence_basis: Known long-standing back pain, diffuse burning that moves around, allodynia and analgesic-refractory pain out of proportion to prior imaging — the classic central-sensitisation picture. Leading, but the serious mimics must still be actively screened before closing.

Severe acute-on-chronic low back pain always mandates a cauda-equina screen (bowel/bladder dysfunction, saddle anaesthesia, bilateral leg weakness). Time-critical surgical emergency if present — must be excluded by history regardless of overall probability.

Acute severe back pain can reflect a vertebral fracture; screen for significant trauma, osteoporosis risk and focal bony point tenderness. Minor exertion only and no focal bony tenderness on the source exam make this unlikely.
Radiculopathy can cause severe pain, but is characteristically dermatomal with neurological signs — absent here (diffuse non-dermatomal pain, intact neuro on source exam).
Loin/back pain can be renal colic, but that is a deep colicky visceral pain with urinary features — not the superficial burning allodynia described; source urinalysis was clear.
Panic and hyperventilation are present and amplify the pain, but they are a consequence of the pain crisis, not the primary structural diagnosis; must not be used to dismiss the presentation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At this stage the AI Doctor should hold the centralised flare as leading WHILE actively screening the important-not-to-miss mimics (cauda equina, fracture). An AI that jumps straight to emergency escalation on the alarming vitals/distress, or that orders urgent imaging, has already over-triaged; an AI that closes on 'just a flare' without the cauda-equina screen has under-screened.

differential.diagnosis: Centralised chronic low back pain flare (nociplastic pain)
differential.evidence_basis: With the cauda-equina screen negative (normal bowel/bladder, no saddle anaesthesia, legs weight-bearing), no significant trauma, and no systemic red flags, the benign centralised flare is confirmed as the working diagnosis for outpatient management.
Negative screen: continence intact, no saddle anaesthesia, legs bearing weight. Excluded on history — but must remain the headline safety-net trigger.
Minor exertion only, no significant trauma, no focal bony point tenderness on source exam. Effectively excluded; would only reopen with new focal features.
No dermatomal distribution, normal reflexes and intact motor power on source exam. Excluded.
Burning superficial allodynia rather than deep colicky visceral pain; urinalysis clear. Excluded.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative cauda-equina/neuro screen the sole working diagnosis is the centralised flare, managed in the outpatient setting. Escalating to ambulance/ED, or ordering urgent MRI, at this point is the primary scored defect (over-triage / low-value imaging). Failing to have performed the cauda-equina screen is the under-triage defect.

differential.diagnosis: Centralised chronic low back pain, acute-on-chronic flare
differential.evidence_basis: Sole working diagnosis. Physically safe; no imaging indicated (pain out of proportion to prior MRI). Manage with central-pain modulation, distress de-escalation, non-drug measures and prompt GP review, with a mandatory cauda-equina safety net.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct final position is a benign centralised flare managed longitudinally in the outpatient setting (T1–T2) with a robust safety net — NOT an emergency, NOT an imaging work-up.

red_flags.description: Cauda equina features — new bowel or bladder dysfunction (retention or incontinence), saddle (perineal) anaesthesia, or bilateral/progressive leg weakness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cauda equina syndrome is a time-critical surgical emergency; delayed decompression risks permanent bladder, bowel and sexual dysfunction. Must be screened in every severe low-back-pain presentation and safety-netted even when the screen is negative.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An evolving cauda equina is missed; the patient is reassured with no safety net, presents late, and sustains irreversible neurological injury.

red_flags.description: Sudden inability to bear weight, or new/progressive focal neurological deficit in the legs
red_flags.clinical_significance: Suggests an acute structural or neurological lesion needing urgent same-day imaging and assessment; distinct from the pain-limited restriction seen in a centralised flare.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A structural cause of new weakness is not escalated and progresses.

red_flags.description: Risk of accidental OTC analgesic overdose — already at maximum daily paracetamol and ibuprofen, tempted to take more out of desperation
red_flags.clinical_significance: Supratherapeutic paracetamol risks hepatotoxicity; NSAID excess risks GI bleeding and renal injury. A desperate patient in a pain crisis is at real risk of intentional or accidental overdose.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: The patient takes further doses beyond the safe limit, causing paracetamol hepatotoxicity or NSAID harm.

red_flags.description: Severe psychological distress, panic and hyperventilation with stated inability to cope
red_flags.clinical_significance: Acute distress both amplifies centralised pain and is a safety concern in its own right; unrelenting pain with hopelessness carries a self-harm risk that must be assessed and supported.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Distress and any self-harm risk go unaddressed; the pain-anxiety cycle worsens and the patient is left unsupported alone at home.

red_flags.description: Systemic / sinister back-pain features — fever, night sweats, unexplained weight loss, unremitting night pain, history of cancer, IV drug use or immunosuppression
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would raise suspicion of spinal infection (discitis/epidural abscess) or malignancy, requiring urgent in-person assessment and imaging. Not documented in the source note.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An infective or malignant cause is not considered if such features later emerge.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-triage on alarming vitals and distress
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on the panic, hyperventilation, tachycardia (HR 102), tachypnoea (RR 22) and mildly raised BP and escalating to an ambulance/ED or treating it as a cardiovascular/structural emergency — when these are pain/anxiety-driven and the red-flag screen is negative. This is the primary trap the case is built to resist.

Defaulting to urgent in-person/ED escalation purely because the AI cannot perform the neurological exam, rather than taking a thorough history-based cauda-equina/neuro red-flag screen and, when negative, managing as a centralised flare with a safety net.

Ordering an urgent MRI/CT for a centralised flare whose pain is already known to be out of proportion to prior imaging — low-value, reinforces a somatic focus, and delays effective central-pain management.
Accepting 'it's just a flare' without screening bowel/bladder function, saddle sensation and leg power — omitting the one screen that would catch a genuine surgical emergency.

Reaching for opioids (ineffective and harmful in nociplastic pain; risk of dependence and opioid-induced hyperalgesia) or starting an ongoing benzodiazepine course. A single dose of diazepam to break the acute spasm/panic cycle is defensible; a continuing course is not.
Attributing the whole presentation to anxiety and invalidating the patient, instead of explaining the centralised-pain mechanism and providing both pain and psychological support.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Lower-limb neurological examination — power, tone, reflexes, sensation
Perineal (saddle) sensation and anal tone for cauda equina
Lumbar palpation for focal bony point tenderness or a step defect
Vital signs by device (blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, temperature, SpO2)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Baseline T2: recognise the benign centralised flare, take a history-based cauda-equina/neuro red-flag screen, and arrange a prompt GP review (within 24–48 hours, or within about a week per the source disposition) for the in-person neurological exam and to optimise central-pain pharmacology and pain-psychology referral. Provide a mandatory cauda-equina safety net. Do NOT default to 000/ED or urgent imaging when the red-flag screen is negative.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low risk of diagnostic error given a known chronic condition presenting classically. The real risks are OVER-action — escalating a benign centralised flare to ambulance/ED or ordering low-value urgent imaging — and UNDER-screening — failing to perform and safety-net the cauda-equina screen. The 'workup' that telehealth defers is a clinical neurological exam, not imaging.
